abstract: While mainstream scholarship often contends that Islam has had little impact on Indonesia’s foreign policy, the nation’s diplomatic history during its revolution for independence suggests otherwise. Between 1945 and 1949, the decentralized nature of the independence struggle allowed non-state actors and civil society to play a direct role in conducting foreign relations. During this pivotal period, Indonesia leveraged Islamic identity as a strategic cornerstone of its diplomatic outreach to the Arab world. While this approach secured crucial early recognition and international support, shifting geopolitical dynamics after 1948 eventually marginalized the role of Islam, leading to a decline in the prominence of Arab-Indonesian relations in the country’s foreign policy agenda.
